architecture.summary: A two-state transformer-based recursive reasoning system (Tiny Recursive Model, TRM) that maintains an embedded answer state y and latent reasoning state z, both updated by a shared parameter backbone and decoded via an output head. Each recursion step produces two output distributions: a reference policy before latent update and an improved policy after latent update. The system is trained with Deep Improvement Supervision (DIS), which provides step-wise intermediate targets via discrete corruption schedules, supervising each recursion step to behave as a policy-improvement operator that maps corrupted targets toward ground truth.
trained_components: transformer backbone (shared for latent and answer updates)

biblio.title: Latent Reasoning in TRMs is Secretly a Policy Improvement Operator

claims.statement: Latent recursive reasoning can be formalized as a policy improvement algorithm: a single TRM step implements a policy improvement update by taking a reference policy (current guess) and transforming it into an improved policy using an internally computed advantage-like signal defined as the log-ratio between post-reasoning and pre-reasoning distributions.

A latent-reasoning update helps exactly when the ground-truth action has an above-average improvement score under the interpolated policy family, formalized as the Advantage Margin condition: the cross-entropy loss decreases if and only if the ground-truth token has above-average improvement score.
On ARC-AGI-1 (800 tasks), the DIS-compact model (0.8 million parameters) achieves 24.0 accuracy pass@2, compared to TRM-compact baseline achieving 12.0 accuracy pass@2.
On ARC-AGI-1, the DIS-medium model (7 million parameters) achieves 40.0 accuracy pass@2, compared to TRM-medium baseline achieving 27.1 accuracy pass@2.
On ARC-AGI-2 (1,120 tasks), the DIS-medium model (7 million parameters) achieves 2.5 accuracy pass@2, compared to TRM-medium baseline achieving 0.0 accuracy pass@2.
Deep Improvement Supervision reduces the total number of forward passes by 18× while maintaining performance on complex reasoning tasks.
On N-Queens reasoning problem, DIS-compact achieves 0.69 accuracy while using 9× fewer latent reasoning steps (T=1, n=2) compared to TRM which uses T=3, n=6.

DIS does not outperform TRM on Sudoku with MLP backbone; the model requires more optimization steps to reach the same final performance, suggesting that stepwise corruption targets may not provide the most effective improvement trajectory for all tasks.
